FOREWORD
The 1954 General Assembly imposed upon the Legislative
Research Commission the duty of furnishing the printer correct
copy of bills and resolutions for inclusion in the Kentucky Acts.
House Bill No. 440 of 1964 added the requirement that the
Legislative Research Commission prepare indexes to all editions
of the Kentucky Acts after proofreading from the enrolled bills.
This volume represents the fulfillment of these obligations.
This edition of the Kentucky Acts continues the practice,
initiated in 1962, of carrying new material in italics and deleted
material in brackets when a bill amends an existing section of
the Kentucky Revised Statutes. By this procedure, members of
the General Assembly and other interested persons may readily
determine changes in existing law without constant referral to
the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
In the preparation of this volume of the Kentucky Acts of
the First Extraordinary Session of 1965 the Secretary of State has
furnished access to copy of filed enrolled bills and resolutions.
The staff of the Legislative Research Commission compared the
prepared page proofs with the enrolled bills. Bills and resolu
tions are inserted by chapters according to the order in which
they received gubernatorial action and were filed in the office of
the Secretary of State.
HARRY LEE WATERFIELD
Chairman
JAMES T.
Director
FLEMING
The Capitol
Frankfort, Kentucky
October 15, 1965

CHAPTER 2
(H. B. 1)
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation.
WHEREAS, the courts of this Commonwealth, in exercise of their
constitutional function, have determined and directed that property
be assessed at its fair cash value; and
WHEREAS, that determination of the courts has been succeeded by
great anxiety and uncertainty among the citizens of this Common
wealth; and
WHEREAS, it is the desire of the General Assembly to offer reassurance
of fiscal responsibility by limiting the revenues derived from ad
valorem taxes to insure that injustices do not devolve upon our
people; and
WHEREAS, it is the function and duty of this General Assembly to
provide the legal framework for fair and equitable taxation to sup
port the services of government which are demanded by its people;
and
WHEREAS, there is widespread determination among the people of this
Commonwealth that our public services, especially the public school
system, shall not be crippled nor impaired by unrealistic or inade
quate support; and
WHEREAS, the purpose of taxation is to insure that adequate, but not
excessive, revenues are produced in support of essential public
services; and
WHEREAS, it is proper and necessary that all taxing districts of this
Commonwealth be permitted to function with the approval and
consent of those governed in a productive and progressive provision
of public services; and
WHEREAS, it is mandatory in our democracy to assure voter partici
pation in the matter of taxation and all public policy; and
WHEREAS, the progress and tranquility of our Commonwealth and
our people can be assured best through a common consideration of
our common problems and their fair and equitable resolution; and
WHEREAS, it is the duty and the mandate of this Special Assembly
to properly and equitably reflect the will of our people to secure
their greatest happiness, security and prosperity.
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:

utes is amended to read as follows:

be taken now so as to make it available to the 1966 General
Assembly and to the people on the same schedule as the other
alternatives;
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Upon the concurrence in this Act by the next
General Assembly the sense of the people of the Commonwealth
shall be taken in the manner prescribed in the Constitution of
the Commonwealth of Kentucky and this Act as to the necessity
and expediency of calling a convention for the purpose of revising

gress of the United States to call a Convention for the purpose

stitution of the United States.
"Article
"Section 1. Nothing in this Constitution shall prohibit any
state which shall have a bicameral legislature from apportioning
the membership of one house of such legislature on factors other
than population, provided that the plan of such apportionment
shall have been submitted to and approved by a vote of the

ing bodies of its subordinate units shall be apportioned.

have been ratified as an amendment to the Constitution by the
legislatures of three-fourths of the Several States within seven
years from the date of its submission to the States by the
Congress."

lution be immediatelv transmitted to the Secretarv of the Senate
